notes: The provided text contains only author names and affiliations with no clinical findings, statistical results, or outcome data to extract.

source_info.section: A multicentre prospective registry of one thousand sepsis patients admitted in Indian ICUs: (SEPSIS INDIA) study

population: Sepsis patients (including septic shock) admitted to ICU, Indian Sepsis Registry
sample_size: N=1172 (septic shock n=500, without shock n=672)
notes: Community-acquired infections predominant; lung most common source; 65% gram negative organisms; 50% carbapenem-resistance in gram negative blood culture isolates

population: Sepsis patients (including septic shock) admitted to ICU, Indian Sepsis Registry
sample_size: N=1172 (septic shock n=500, without shock n=672)
notes: Community-acquired infections predominant; lung most common source; 65% gram negative organisms; 50% carbapenem-resistance in gram negative blood culture isolates

population: Sepsis patients (including septic shock) admitted to ICU, Indian Sepsis Registry
sample_size: N=1172 (septic shock n=500, without shock n=672)
notes: Community-acquired infections predominant; lung most common source; 65% gram negative organisms; 50% carbapenem-resistance in gram negative blood culture isolates

population: Sepsis patients admitted to ICU in India
performance: Comparatively better predictor of poor outcome
notes: Gram negative infection was predominant cause of sepsis with high incidence of carbapenem resistance. Escherichia coli, Klebsiella Spp and Acinetobacter Spp were predominant causative organisms. Tropical infections constituted a minority of sepsis population with low hospital mortality.

population: Sepsis patients admitted to ICU in India
predictor: Nosocomial infection as source of sepsis
effect_size: Worst outcomes associated with nosocomial infections
notes: Source control, correct empirical antibiotic selection, and intravenous thiamine were identified as protective factors.

population: Sepsis patients admitted to ICU in India
predictor: Source control, correct empirical antibiotic selection, and intravenous thiamine
notes: CTRI Registration: CTRI:2022/07/044516. These factors were identified as protective against poor outcomes in sepsis.

population: ICU patients with sepsis or septic shock (Sepsis-3 criteria), 19 participating centres

notes: Predicted mortality rate (APACHE II): overall 40%, sepsis 25%, septic shock 55%; SMR: overall 0.9, sepsis 1.02, septic shock 0.92

population: ICU patients with sepsis or septic shock
sample_size: N=1172 (septic shock n=500, sepsis without shock n=672)
effect_size: Septic shock mean APACHE II 26.22 (SD 14.36) vs Sepsis mean APACHE II 18.63 (SD 9.62)
notes: Observed hospital mortality: total cohort 36.34% (n=426), septic shock 50.8% (n=254), sepsis without shock 25.6% (n=172). Predicted hospital mortality (per mean APACHE II): total 40%, septic shock 55%, sepsis 25%. Standardised mortality ratio (SMR): total 0.9, septic shock 0.92, sepsis 1.024.

population: ICU patients with sepsis or septic shock
sample_size: N=1172 (septic shock n=500, sepsis without shock n=672)
effect_size: Septic shock mean SOFA 8.30 (SD 3.78) vs Sepsis mean SOFA 5.51 (SD 3.02); total cohort mean 6.70 (SD 3.64)
notes: SOFA score reported as demographic/severity descriptor; no p-value provided for this variable in the table.

population: ICU patients with sepsis or septic shock
sample_size: N=1172 (septic shock n=500, sepsis without shock n=672)
effect_size: Total cohort mean 3.52 (SD 2.46); septic shock mean 3.50 (SD 2.67); sepsis mean 3.53 (SD 2.29)
notes: No statistically significant difference between groups reported for this variable.

population: ICU patients with sepsis or septic shock
sample_size: N=1172 (septic shock n=500, sepsis without shock n=672)
effect_size: Blood most common: total 41.6%, septic shock 46.9%, sepsis 36.9%; Respiratory tract: total 24.0%, septic shock 19.2%, sepsis 28.2%; Urine: total 25.0%, septic shock 22.1%, sepsis 27.5%
notes: Community-acquired infection: total 81.9%, septic shock 76.8%, sepsis 85.7%. Other sites include CNS, Heart, Bones/Joints, Female Reproductive System, Head/Neck.
Factors significantly associated with better outcomes included source control, correct empirical antibiotic choice, and loading dose of antibiotic.
Empiric antibiotic choice was correct in 62% of patients when compared to subsequent culture results.

predictor: Administration of a loading dose of antibiotic

performance: Not significantly associated with better outcomes
Not significantly associated with better outcomes

population: ICU patients with sepsis infected with carbapenem-resistant organism
effect_size: 31% mortality in carbapenem-resistant vs 21% in carbapenem-sensitive
notes: Carbapenem-resistant organisms detected in 57% of all positive blood cultures. Gram-negative organisms constituted 80% of all positive blood cultures.

predictor: Risk factors for drug-resistant bacterial infections

population: Sepsis patients with tropical infections (Dengue, Malaria, Typhus) within the sepsis cohort
notes: Average age 45 years (SD 20), 65% male, SOFA 4.41, APACHE II 8.75, average length of stay 6 days; much lower mortality than general septic cohort

Sepsis and septic shock patients in ICU
notes: SOFA consistently found to be the best discriminator for hospital mortality across studies

population: Sepsis patients discharged alive from hospital
effect_size: Average ICU LOS 8 days; average hospital LOS 11 days for sepsis

population: Septic shock patients discharged alive from hospital
effect_size: Average ICU LOS 8 days; average hospital LOS 11 days for septic shock
SMR 0.9 for sepsis; SMR 0.92 for septic shock
notes: Improvement from previous Indian study which reported SMR of 1.4

predictor: Nosocomial infections associated with sepsis or organ failure
notes: Empirical choice of appropriate antibiotic and adequate source control also identified as independent predictors of mortality.
Authors note many patients are malnourished, emphasising importance of nutritional screening and management upon admission.
Independent use of IV thiamine (not in combination with vitamin C and hydrocortisone). Literature on independent use of thiamine in sepsis is sparse.

population: Minority subset of sepsis patients with tropical infections
performance: Lower than general septic population; higher than overall study cohort
notes: Demographics and outcomes comparable to INDICAP II point prevalence study; similar age and SOFA score reported.

population: Patients with sepsis and septic shock admitted to ICUs in India (sepsis 3 criteria)
performance: Best predictive score for hospital mortality among scores evaluated
notes: Multicentre, prospective registry study. Hospital mortality was 25% in sepsis and 50% in septic shock. One third of infections were due to Carbapenem-resistant Gram-negative organisms. Nosocomial infection had the worst odds for mortality.
